title: Mud Kitchen
summary: An outdoor play kitchen from three 2x2s, one 2x4, and four cedar pickets — with a cut-in sink for a real wash bin and a backsplash shelf for the mud-pie supplies.
description:
A mud kitchen is a play kitchen that's supposed to get filthy, which changes every design decision. It lives outside, so it's cedar pickets and gapped shelves that drain instead of plywood that swells. It gets water dumped on it daily, so the 'sink' is a hole cut in the counter that a plastic bin drops into — lift the bin out, dump it, done. And it's for kids, so it's counter-height for a five-year-old and every edge gets sanded round.

The frame is three ladder-style leg sets — tall 2x2 back legs (40"), short front legs (19-1/4"), connected by 2x4 and 2x2 rails — joined front and back into a sturdy little bench with a backsplash rising behind. The upper shelf hangs on the backsplash on 45°-cut 2x2 brackets, putting the play dishes and potion jars at kid eye level.

One cut deserves your full attention, and the source flags it too: the three little 45° bracket blocks are only 7" long, and cutting small pieces on a saw is how hands get hurt. Cut them from the END of a long 2x2 — never from a short offcut — and clamp the work, every time. No exceptions on a project you're building WITH a kid watching.

Size the sink cutout to the bin you actually bought, traced upside down and cut about 1/2" inside the rim line so the lip hangs on the counter. Finish with something kid-safe and penetrating (or leave the cedar raw to silver); skip film finishes that flake into the mud soup.

tools.note: The sink cutout and the optional stovetop rings.
Pilot holes in 2x2 stock (it splits), and the 1/2" starter holes for the jigsaw cuts.
Every edge, twice. Small hands live on this thing.
For the 45° bracket cuts — clamped, from a long board. A circular saw and clamps also work.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
materials.note: Counter, bottom shelf, and upper shelf.

materials.name: Trim screws, 2-1/2", self-tapping
materials.note: Frame joints. Trim screws' small heads sit clean in 2x2 stock.

materials.name: Plastic wash bin
materials.note: Buy the bin FIRST — the sink hole is traced from it.

materials.name: Kid-safe penetrating finish
materials.note: Or leave the cedar raw. No film finishes — they flake into the play.

cutList.note: They run past the counter to carry the backsplash and upper shelf.
45° both ends, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel. Cut from the end of a LONG board, clamped — never from a short scrap.

steps.title: Build three leg sets
steps.body:
Each leg set is a tall back leg and a short front leg joined by a 2x4 counter support up top and a 2x2 bottom-shelf rail lower down. Build all three identical — two screws into the 2x4s, one per 2x2 joint.

Pre-drill every screw in this build: 2x2 stock splits near its ends without a pilot hole, and nearly every joint here lands near an end.

steps.title: Join the leg sets
steps.body: Connect the three leg sets with a front rail and a back rail, two screws per joint, the middle leg set centered. Stand it on flat ground and check for rock before the final screws — a three-legged-table wobble is much easier to fix now than after the counter's on.

steps.title: Complete the frame
steps.body: Add the remaining 2x4 supports — the rest of the counter bearers and the backsplash rail across the tall legs. Everything the pickets land on should now be in place.

steps.title: Lay the bottom shelf
steps.body: Lay picket boards across the bottom rails, notching the front board around the legs with the jigsaw. Screw down with even gaps between boards — the gaps are the drainage plan, and a mud kitchen without drainage is a mosquito farm.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

steps.title: Counter, then sink
steps.body:
Screw the counter pickets down with even gaps. Then trace your actual wash bin upside down where the sink goes, draw a cut line about 1/2" INSIDE the traced rim, drill a 1/2" starter hole, and jigsaw the opening. Drop the bin in — the rim hangs on the counter, and lifts out for dumping.

Trace the real bin, not a measurement of it. Bins taper and their rims aren't square, and a hole cut to the catalog dimension is how bins fall through counters.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

steps.title: Backsplash and upper shelf
steps.body:
Screw the backsplash board across the tall legs, flush to their tops. Attach the shelf board to the bottom edge of the backsplash, then fit the three 45° brackets under it — the brackets carry the real load when the shelf gets leaned on, which it will.

Cut those brackets from the end of a long 2x2, clamped to the saw. Seven-inch pieces and fingers do not share a saw fence.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

steps.title: Stovetop, sanding, finish
steps.body:
If the chef wants burners, cut rings from picket scraps — drill a 1/2" hole and connect the circle with the jigsaw — and screw them to the counter.

Then the most important step on a kids' build: sand everything, round every corner, and run your bare palm over every surface a kid will touch. Finish with a kid-safe penetrating oil or let the cedar silver. Stock with pots, spoons, and a strict no-mud-in-the-house treaty.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

images.alt: Cedar outdoor play mud kitchen with sink bin, backsplash shelf, and lower shelf
